docTitle: Comparison of Aluminum, Stainless Steel and Titanium Alloy Selection | News | Agile Intelligent Manufacturing
mainHtml:
Resources / Newsroom / Material Selection
MATERIAL COMPARISON

Comparison of all-round selection of aluminum alloy, stainless steel, and titanium alloys, three commonly used machined metals

Three metals cover precision parts scenarios such as automation equipment, automotive, medical, and aerospace. This paper compares the differences horizontally from the dimensions of physical properties, processing difficulty, applicable scenarios, surface treatment and processing costs.

Material Selection Aluminum alloy Stainless steel Aluminum Alloy
MATERIAL COMPARISON

The choice of aluminum alloy, stainless steel, titanium alloy is essentially a balance between performance and cost

Aluminum alloys, stainless steel and titanium alloys are three types of metal materials that are very common in precision machining. They cover a large number of parts scenarios such as automation equipment, automobiles, medical care, aerospace, etc. However, there are significant differences in weight, strength, corrosion resistance, cutting difficulty and processing cost. If the material selection stage only focuses on a single indicator, it will often lead to redundant performance or out-of-control costs.

This article is for R&D engineers, structural designers, material engineers, procurement staff and project evaluation teams
00 Selection overview 01 Physical 02 Processing Difficulty 03 Applicable Scenario 04 Surface Finishing 05 Cost Balance
00
SELECTION OVERVIEW

Typical positioning of three types of metallic materials

Aluminum alloy is more suitable for lightweight, heat dissipation and high-efficiency processing scenarios; Stainless steel is more suitable for parts with high corrosion resistance, rigidity and appearance requirements; Titanium alloys are used for projects with higher requirements for contrast strength, corrosion resistance, biocompatibility and high-end working conditions.

At the project design stage, blind selection of the highest performing or highest priced materials is not recommended. Reasonable material selection should revolve around four questions: Do parts need to be lightweight?Is it subjected to higher loads? Is it in a humid, acid, alkali, or corrosive environment?Are there clear limits on processing costs and lead times?

Materials Key benefits Main Restrictions Typical Positioning
Aluminum alloy 6061 / 7075 Light weight, good thermal conductivity, high processing efficiency Weak rigidity, thin wall parts are easy to deform Lightweight structure, housing, module, radiator
Stainless steel 304 / 316 Corrosion resistance, good rigidity, wide application The work hardening is obvious, and the tool loss is large. Corrosion resistant parts, body, fasteners
Aluminum Alloy TC4 High specific strength, corrosion resistance, good biocompatibility Difficult to cut, high processing costs Medical, aerospace, high-end corrosion-resistant components
01
PHYSICAL PERFORMANCE

Basic physical properties: significant differences in weight, strength and corrosion resistance

Aluminum alloy has the lightest weight, about one-third the density of stainless steel, and excellent heat dissipation. Conventional 6061-T6 can meet most structural strength requirements, and 7075-T6 high-strength aluminum alloy can be adapted to heavy load conditions. Therefore, aluminum alloys generally have a high comprehensive cost performance ratio in automation equipment, new energy shells and lightweight brackets.

Stainless steel performs evenly in terms of rigidity and corrosion resistance. The 304 is suitable for normal humid environments and the 316 is resistant to harsher acid and alkali corrosion environments. Stainless steels are generally more stable than regular carbon steels and aluminum alloys if the part requires prolonged exposure to moisture, water or near chemical media.

The specific strength of titanium alloy is high, the weight is lower than that of stainless steel under the same strength, and the corrosion resistance is outstanding in the three types of materials. In addition, titanium alloys have excellent biocompatibility and are therefore commonly used in medical implant components and high-end medical device structures.

02
MACHINABILITY

Difficulty of machining process: the more difficult the material is to cut, the more likely it is that costs and cycles will rise

Aluminum alloy has the best cutting performance.It has high cutting speed, small tool loss, good chip removal performance, Therefore, the processing efficiency is high and the cost is relatively low.For parts with complex structures but moderate strength requirements, Aluminum alloys are often the easier option to control quotations and lead times.

Stainless steel has poor thermal conductivity and is prone to work hardening, slow processing speed and fast tool wear. Under the same structural and precision requirements, the processing cost of stainless steel is usually significantly higher than that of aluminum alloy. The original manuscript mentions that the processing cost is about 1.5 to 2 times that of aluminum parts.

Titanium alloys are typically difficult to cut materials.The cutting heat is easily concentrated in the tip position, If the tool, cooling and parameters do not match, it is easy to burn out the tool and affect the surface quality. Therefore, the processing cost of titanium alloys is generally the highest of the three commonly used metals.

03
APPLICATION SCENARIOS

Scenario: Material selection based on weight, environment and functional requirements

  1. 01

    Aluminum: lightweight and heat dissipation preferred

    Suitable for new energy enclosures, automation modules, equipment cooling parts and lightweight brackets. Aluminum alloys are generally more suitable if the project emphasizes weight control, processing efficiency and appearance anodizing effects.

  2. 02

    Stainless steel: Corrosion resistance and rigidity requirements are more prominent

    Suitable for water environment accessories, corrosion resistant valve body, standard fasteners and appearance decoration structural parts. Stainless steel is more stable if the part is in a humid, corroded or cleaned environment.

  3. 03

    Titanium: High-end strength, corrosion resistance and medical scenarios

    Suitable for aviation structural parts, medical surgical accessories, implantable parts and high-end corrosion-resistant precision parts. However, higher processing costs and longer process evaluation cycles need to be accepted.

04
SURFACE FINISHING

Surface treatment: Different materials correspond to different appearances and protection schemes

Aluminum alloys can be treated with anodic oxidation, hard oxidation, sandblast coloring, etc., A wide selection of exterior colors also enhances the surface's ability to withstand abrasion and corrosion. Therefore, many exterior structural parts and equipment housings prefer aluminum alloys.

Stainless steels often use polishing, passivation, etc. to enhance their appearance and rust resistance. Stainless steel reprocessing options are more mature for parts that require higher food, medical, humid environments, or cleaning.

Titanium alloys can be treated with micro-arc oxidation to further improve wear resistance and anti-corrosion performance. However, the material and processing cost of the titanium alloy itself are relatively high. Whether or not to use it should be judged in combination with the actual working conditions and budget.

05
COST BALANCE

“Excess performance” and “out-of-control costs” should be avoided at the material selection stage

In the project design phase, material selection should be prioritized to match the requirements of the working conditions. If the part only assumes the role of ordinary support, but chooses high-end materials that are difficult to process, it may cause obvious performance redundancy; If a part is in a corrosive environment and is selected only at the lowest cost, there may be a risk of inadequate life and rework.

Reasonable material selection processes should first confirm the use environment, stress requirements, weight requirements, surface treatment methods, and budget boundaries. Then further match the specific material grades. Controlling the cost of materials and processing under the premise of meeting performance standards is a goal that the R&D and procurement teams need to pay attention to together.

MATERIAL DECISION

There is no absolute quality of the material, only whether it is suitable for the current working conditions of the parts

Aluminum alloys are suitable for lightweight, high-efficiency processing and appearance anodization requirements; Stainless steel is suitable for corrosion resistance and structural stability requirements; Titanium alloys are suitable for high-end scenarios such as high specific strength, high corrosion resistance and medical aviation.

Agile Intelligent Manufacturing can be based on drawing usage requirements, stress data, and usage environment. Provide material grade recommendations and process suggestions for the project.

Material Reference

Jiangsu Hanfeng CNC material selection article, Sohu metal processing technology article, Makerstage foreign language material literature.
